worth_it_explanation: Supply chain transparency checks confirm that spend reaches publishers who have authorized The Trade Desk to buy their inventory and that no unauthorized resellers or made-for-advertising (MFA) properties sit in the path. The Trade Desk surfaces SupplyChain object completeness and seller authorization signals in the platform UI. A practitioner can audit the key settings, apply appropriate exclusion lists, and confirm OpenPath direct paths are prioritized in under an hour. The return is reduced waste on low-quality or fraudulent supply and a defensible record of where the budget ran.
intro: When The Trade Desk buys programmatic inventory, each bid request can carry a SupplyChain object that lists every entity in the path from publisher to buyer. The ads.txt and sellers.json standards let buyers cross-reference whether each seller is authorized by the publisher it claims to represent. OpenPath gives The Trade Desk direct integration with major publishers, reducing the reseller hops where fraud risk concentrates. These checks confirm that supply chain transparency tools are applied, that made-for-advertising and low-quality inventory is excluded, and that spend follows paths through verified, authorized sellers.

audit_checks.title: Supply chain transparency is not enforced: unauthorized and resold inventory reaches spend
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without supply chain transparency enforcement, spend flows to sellers who may not be authorized by the publishers they claim to represent. Reseller chains obscure who actually sold the impression, and a portion of budget reaches fraudulent or misrepresented inventory rather than the publisher the targeting selected.

educate.base:
Every bid request The Trade Desk receives can carry a SupplyChain object, defined by the IAB Tech Lab OpenRTB SupplyChain Object specification. The object lists each node in the selling path: the advertising system domain, the seller identifier, and whether that seller is the direct publisher or a reseller. A complete chain lets a buyer trace every hop from publisher to exchange. An incomplete chain signals that one or more entities chose not to disclose their participation.

The complementary standard, sellers.json, lets buyers verify that the seller ID in each chain node is actually listed by the exchange as an authorized seller. When a seller ID in the chain does not appear in the exchange's sellers.json file, or appears as a reseller rather than a publisher, the impression may not originate from the domain the bid request names.

The Trade Desk applies supply chain quality signals natively within Kokai and exposes them as targeting controls. OpenPath direct integrations skip the reseller chain entirely by connecting The Trade Desk to publishers without an intermediary SSP hop. For inventory bought through SSPs, the platform can favor supply paths with complete chains and deprioritize paths where the chain is absent or shows unauthorized resale.

A few signs point to supply chain enforcement being absent or incomplete:
  • No supply quality or supply path controls are active on the ad group
  • The campaign does not prioritize OpenPath inventory for publisher categories where OpenPath coverage exists
  • Third-party verification reports show a meaningful share of spend on supply with incomplete SupplyChain objects
You may want to confirm supply chain enforcement is applied at the ad group level rather than relying on exchange-level filtering alone.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For ecommerce brands running display or video, low-quality or misrepresented supply produces impressions that report a delivery but reach no real user. A checkout-intent audience that buys inventory through an unauthorized reseller chain may generate clicks that never convert. You may want to prioritize OpenPath for the publisher categories most relevant to your category and confirm supply chain controls are on before scaling budgets.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Lead-generation campaigns that pay on a cost-per-lead basis are a target for invalid traffic concentrated in supply with incomplete or fabricated chain data. You may want to confirm supply chain enforcement is active and review whether any traffic sources flagged by a third-party verification vendor correspond to reseller-heavy supply paths.

learn_more.source_title: Sellers.json and the OpenRTB SupplyChain Object

investigate.intro: Confirm that supply chain quality controls are active on the ad groups under review and that OpenPath is being used where available for the relevant publisher categories.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the campaign and navigate to the ad group. In the Inventory section, review whether supply quality filters or supply path optimizations are applied. Note whether any supply-chain-related settings are set to 'off' or left at default with no active selection.

Check whether OpenPath is enabled for the ad group's inventory targets. In Kokai, OpenPath direct paths appear as a selectable inventory type within the supply settings. Note which publisher categories have OpenPath coverage for this campaign's targeting.

Review any third-party brand safety or fraud verification segments attached to the ad group. A verification partner integration such as DoubleVerify or Integral Ad Science (IAS) can surface supply path quality signals in addition to The Trade Desk's native controls.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad group, whether supply chain quality controls are active, whether OpenPath is enabled where publisher coverage exists, and whether a third-party verification partner is applied. An ad group with supply chain controls on, OpenPath prioritized, and a verification segment active passes. An ad group with none of these active, or with supply chain filtering explicitly off, is a fail recorded with the ad group name and the missing control.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the supply chain control status, OpenPath enablement, and verification partner status for each ad group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that supply chain transparency controls are not fully enforced on {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk campaigns. Without active supply chain quality filters and OpenPath prioritization, spend flows to inventory where the selling path may include unauthorized resellers or entities not listed in exchange sellers.json files, increasing the share of budget that reaches misrepresented or fraudulent supply. {{voice_recommend}} enabling supply chain quality controls at the ad group level, activating OpenPath for publisher categories where coverage exists, and attaching a third-party verification partner such as DoubleVerify or IAS to surface ongoing supply path signals. After the changes, review delivery reports for shifts in the share of spend attributed to direct and authorized paths.

audit_checks.title: Made-for-advertising domains are not excluded from inventory targeting
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Made-for-advertising (MFA) sites generate high volumes of low-quality ad calls, often using thin or aggregated content to collect programmatic revenue. Spend on MFA inventory produces impressions with no real audience value, inflates reported delivery metrics, and diverts budget from publishers whose content audiences chose to read.

educate.base:
MFA sites are built to attract programmatic ad spend rather than to serve an audience with editorial content. They acquire traffic through paid content recommendation widgets, display high ratios of ad units to content, and refresh ads rapidly to generate impression volume. Because they run at scale in programmatic auctions, MFA inventory reaches buyers who have not taken steps to exclude it.

The Trade Desk provides native exclusion controls in Kokai that allow advertisers to block categories of low-quality supply. Third-party verification vendors such as DoubleVerify and Integral Ad Science classify MFA domains in pre-bid segments that can be applied to ad groups before a bid is submitted. Using both The Trade Desk's native controls and a pre-bid MFA exclusion segment reduces the share of spend reaching these sites.

The ads.txt standard does not by itself flag MFA domains. An MFA site can implement ads.txt correctly while still producing low-quality inventory. MFA exclusion requires a separate classification layer, either from a verification vendor or from a curated blocklist maintained by the advertiser or agency.

A few signs point to MFA inventory reaching the campaign:
  • No pre-bid MFA exclusion segment is applied at the ad group level
  • Post-campaign reports from a verification vendor show a meaningful share of impressions classified as MFA
  • The campaign's site list includes domains with high ad-to-content ratios and little recognizable editorial identity
You may want to apply a pre-bid MFA exclusion before scaling a campaign rather than reviewing the site list after the fact.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: Ecommerce campaigns buying display retargeting are frequently surfaced on MFA sites because retargeting audiences are among the most valuable to DSPs and MFA publishers compete aggressively for them. You may want to confirm a pre-bid MFA exclusion is active on retargeting ad groups before reviewing attribution results. Conversions driven by MFA impressions are unlikely to represent real purchase intent.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Lead-generation campaigns buying broad awareness inventory face particular MFA exposure because audience targeting criteria that favor high reach also match the traffic profiles MFA sites generate. You may want to apply a verification-vendor MFA pre-bid segment and review the post-campaign site report for any domains that account for disproportionate impression share relative to their known reach.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether a pre-bid MFA exclusion segment is applied to the ad groups under review and whether post-campaign reports show MFA classified impressions.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the ad group and navigate to the Audience or Inventory section. Look for any pre-bid brand safety or fraud exclusion segments applied. Note whether a segment specifically targeting MFA exclusion from a verification vendor is listed.

Review the campaign's delivery report or site-level breakdown if available. Identify any domains that account for a large share of impressions but are unfamiliar as recognizable editorial publishers. A domain serving high impression volume with low recognizable brand identity warrants investigation.

If a third-party verification partner such as DoubleVerify or IAS is active on the account, pull their campaign report and look for the MFA classification metric. Note the percentage of impressions flagged and the top domains contributing to that share.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad group, whether a pre-bid MFA exclusion segment is applied and whether post-campaign reporting from a verification partner shows MFA-classified impressions above a threshold the advertiser or agency considers acceptable. An ad group with a pre-bid MFA exclusion active and no material MFA share in post-campaign reports passes. An ad group with no pre-bid exclusion and post-campaign MFA share above threshold is a fail recorded with the ad group name, the verification vendor's MFA percentage, and the top contributing domains.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the pre-bid MFA exclusion status and, where available, the verification vendor's MFA impression share for each ad group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that made-for-advertising (MFA) inventory exclusions are not applied on {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk campaigns. MFA sites generate high volumes of programmatic impressions with no real audience value, and without a pre-bid exclusion segment a share of budget reaches these properties rather than publishers with genuine editorial audiences. {{voice_recommend}} applying a pre-bid MFA exclusion segment from a verification vendor such as DoubleVerify or Integral Ad Science to the affected ad groups, and reviewing the post-campaign site report to identify any MFA-heavy domains already in the delivery history. After the change, pull the verification vendor's report to confirm the MFA impression share has declined to an acceptable level.
